flashcards.front: What is the root/base class of the entire C++ stream class hierarchy?
flashcards.back: ios_base. It provides platform-independent interface for all stream operations including format flags, locale settings, and error handling state.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the ios class in C++ stream hierarchy?
flashcards.back: The ios class (derived from ios_base) provides common input/output functionality including stream state flags (good, bad, fail, eof), format control, and association with streambuf objects.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between istream and ostream classes?
flashcards.back: istream provides input operations (>> operator, get(), read(), etc.), while ostream provides output operations (<< operator, put(), write(), etc.). They are complementary base classes.

flashcards.front: What is iostream class and what is its purpose?
flashcards.back: iostream is a dual-purpose class derived from both istream and ostream. It provides both input and output capabilities. Classes like fstream inherit from iostream to support bidirectional operations.

flashcards.front: Name the three main file stream classes in C++ and their purposes.
flashcards.back: ifstream (input file stream - reading), ofstream (output file stream - writing), and fstream (file stream - both reading and writing). All are derived from iostream.

flashcards.front: What is the role of streambuf class in the C++ I/O system?
flashcards.back: streambuf is the buffer management class that handles the physical storage and retrieval of characters. It provides the low-level interface between the stream and the actual I/O device.

flashcards.front: What does the ios::app file opening mode do?
flashcards.back: ios::app (append mode) opens the file for output but positions the write pointer at the end of the file. All output is appended to the existing content.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between cin, cout, and cerr objects?
flashcards.back: cin is a istream object connected to standard input (keyboard), cout is an ostream connected to standard output (console), and cerr is an ostream connected to standard error output (unbuffered).

flashcards.front: What are stream manipulators and give two examples?
flashcards.back: Stream manipulators are functions that modify stream behavior. Examples: endl (outputs newline and flushes buffer), setw(n) (sets field width), setprecision(n), fixed, hex.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between text mode and binary mode file opening?
flashcards.back: In text mode, newline characters are translated (\n to \r\n on Windows). In binary mode, data is written/read exactly as stored without any translation. Use ios::binary for binary mode.

flashcards.front: What does the eof() function return and when does it become true?
flashcards.back: eof() returns true when the End-Of-File indicator is set, which happens AFTER an attempt to read past the end of file. It does NOT predict end-of-file before a read fails.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between istream, ostream, iostream, and the file stream classes?
flashcards.back: ifstream inherits from istream, ofstream inherits from ostream, and fstream inherits from iostream. They add file-specific functionality while reusing stream I/O capabilities.
